title: Prepare for a Power Outage
summary: Gather a small kit and make a simple plan for when the power goes out.
learnerPromise: You will be able to put together a basic outage kit and know what to do during a power cut to stay safe and comfortable for several hours.
whyItMatters: Power outages can happen during storms, heat waves, or grid failures. A small amount of preparation helps you stay calm, find supplies in the dark, and keep your phone charged for communication.
realLifeUses: Build a kit with a flashlight, batteries, and a phone charger for storm season
Know where your circuit breaker is and how to check if the outage is just your home
Keep a list of emergency numbers and your utility company's outage line
Plan where to go if the outage lasts longer than a few hours and temperatures are extreme
materialsNeeded: Portable phone charger or power bank
Small container or bag for the kit
result: A ready-to-use power outage kit stored in an easy-to-find location, plus a short plan.
youWillLearn: Assemble a kit with light, power, and communication supplies
Check whether an outage affects only your home or the wider area
Find and safely reset a tripped circuit breaker
Decide when to call the utility company or leave for a safer location
miniChallenge: Gather a flashlight, a portable charger, and one other useful item into a bag or drawer. Label it 'outage kit' and tell someone in your home where it is.
steps: Find a bag or box and label it clearly as your outage kit
Add a flashlight, extra batteries, and a fully charged portable phone charger
Write down the utility company's outage number and a few emergency contacts on paper
Find your circuit breaker and learn which switch controls which area
Store the kit in a place everyone in the home can find in the dark
completionCriteria: Your outage kit is assembled and stored in an agreed, easy-to-find location
You know where your circuit breaker is and can describe how to check it
commonProblems: Portable chargers are often dead because they were not recharged after last use
Flashlight batteries leak if left in the device for long periods
It can be hard to tell if the outage is just your home or the whole neighborhood
tips: Check and recharge your power bank every few months
Store batteries separately from the flashlight to prevent corrosion
Keep a small amount of cash in the kit since card readers may not work
If you rely on medical devices, talk to your doctor about a backup power plan
helpBoundary: Call your utility company if the outage lasts more than a few hours, if you see downed power lines, or if someone in your home relies on electric medical equipment. For extended outages during extreme heat or cold, consider going to a public shelter or a friend's home.
